coredata.dc:title: Amino-terminated carbon dots for imaging Golgi apparatus polarity in live cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Lei
Hou, Peng
Chen, Na
Li, Yuan Fang
Li, Chun Mei
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Cellular polarity plays a crucial role in numerous pathological and biological processes. For example, the polarity of the Golgi apparatus strongly influences the intracellular protein processing and transport. The development of fluorescent probes for tracking of subcellular levels of polarity changes remains a great challenge. Herein, amino-terminated carbon dots (CDs) which have superior Golgi apparatus targeting ability and polarity-sensitive property were designed and fabricated by the mixing of p-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine through a simple solvothermal route. CDs allowed effectively imaging of Golgi apparatus morphological dynamics during cell division and cell death, while folic acid-conjugated CDs possessed high folate-receptor binding affinity and could be used to identify cancer cells. Importantly, the Golgi polarity in apoptotic cells was found to be higher than that of non-apoptotic cells. This work provided a reliable and robust way to real-time track the changes of Golgi apparatus and the local polarity variation, implying the great potential of CDs for the apoptosis evaluation and therapeutic drug screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Golgi apparatus targeting
Polarity analysis
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Intracellular imaging of golgi-polarity changes during apoptosis
FL imaging of the Golgi apparatus
Morphological dynamics of the Golgi apparatus
Polarity imaging of the Golgi apparatus
CDs-1y modified with FA for imaging the Golgi of cancer cell

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976144 and 22134005 ), and Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of the preparation of CDs. (A) One-pot synthesis and purification route for CDs. (B) Synthetic routes with different carbon sources, designated as CDs-1 to CDs-6.

Polarity-dependent emission of CDs-1y. (A) Photographs of the emission colors of CDs-1y in different solvents under excitation by a 365-nm UV lamp (1, toluene; 2, chloroform; 3, DMSO; 4, ethanol; 5, methanol; 6, water). (B) FL emission spectra of CDs-1y in different organic solvents. (C) Plot between the polarity (Δf) of different solvents and the Stokes shift (Δλ) of CDs-1y in solvents. Inset: schematic of the FL emission from controllable surface states of CDs-1y triggered by solvent polarity. (D) FL emission spectra of CD-1y/polymer materials. Inset: photographs of CDs-1y/polymer materials under excitation by a UV lamp.

Cell imaging of various CDs. (A) Schematic of different cells incubated with CDs-1y and Golgi tracker. (B) Confocal FL images of various cells stained with CDs-1y and BODIPY TR C5-ceramide (Golgi tracker red), scale bar: 20 μm. (C) Confocal fluorescence images of A549 after incubation with various CDs for 4 h, scale bar: 20 μm. All cells were incubated with CDs (50 μg mL−1) for 4 h. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Change in the polarity of the Golgi apparatus during cell apoptosis. Images at channels of DIC60, Fura340 (460–510 nm), GFP475 (510–550 nm), and CY3 (570–650 nm) were captured after treatment with DOX for 0 (A), 6 (B), or 12 h (C), scale bar: 10 μm. (D), (E), and (F) represent the FL intensities of Fura340, GFP475, and CY3 after treatment with DOX for 0, 6, and 12 h, respectively. The cell was pretreated by incubating with CDs-1y containing 50 μg mL−1 of the cell culture (i.e., 2% FBS) for 4 h.

Polarity-sensitive response and cell imaging of CDs-5. (A) FL spectra of CDs-5 in various solvents. Inset: photographs of CDs-5 under a 365-nm UV lamp in different solvents (from left to right: ethyl acetate, DMSO, ethanol, methanol, and water). (B) FL spectra of CDs-5 (0.07 mg mL−1) in water/1,4-dioxane solvent mixtures (the percentage indicates 1,4-dioxane content). Inset: linearity of FL intensity versus Δf. (C) Cell imaging of CDs-5 preincubated with the A549 cell after treatment with UV irradiation, scale bar: 20 μm. (D) Change in the FL intensity of CDs-5 in (C).

FL imaging of the CDs-1y-folate conjugate. (A) Synthesis of the y-CDs-FA conjugate. (B) Cell images of the CD-1y-folate conjugate in HUVEC and HeLa, scale bar: 30 μm. (C) FL intensity profile of regions of interest in (B). (D) Mean FL intensity of treated cells.

head.ce:title: Amino-terminated carbon dots for imaging Golgi apparatus polarity in live cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University), Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University), Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellular polarity plays a crucial role in numerous pathological and biological processes. For example, the polarity of the Golgi apparatus strongly influences the intracellular protein processing and transport. The development of fluorescent probes for tracking of subcellular levels of polarity changes remains a great challenge. Herein, amino-terminated carbon dots (CDs) which have superior Golgi apparatus targeting ability and polarity-sensitive property were designed and fabricated by the mixing of p-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine through a simple solvothermal route. CDs allowed effectively imaging of Golgi apparatus morphological dynamics during cell division and cell death, while folic acid-conjugated CDs possessed high folate-receptor binding affinity and could be used to identify cancer cells. Importantly, the Golgi polarity in apoptotic cells was found to be higher than that of non-apoptotic cells. This work provided a reliable and robust way to real-time track the changes of Golgi apparatus and the local polarity variation, implying the great potential of CDs for the apoptosis evaluation and therapeutic drug screening.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In biological systems, the polarity plays central roles in establishing and maintaining specialized domains’ structure and function at the cellular level in particular [1]. Various fundamental physiological processes such as cell differentiation, signal transduction, localized membrane growth, and directional molecules transport are closely related to the cell polarity dynamics [2–4]. Growing evidences proved that defects in cell polarity are responsible for embryonic lethality, inflammation, and tumorigenesis [5,6]. To better understand the polarity functions in physiological and pathological processes, designing novel fluorescent probes for visualizing cell polarity changes, especially the intracellular organelle polarity variation has received tremendous attention because of the simple operation, high sensitivity and temporal-spatial resolution, and real-time monitoring [7]. Accumulating polarity-sensitive organic fluorescent probes have been constructed and utilized to distinguish polarity in organelles such as lipid droplets [8,9], mitochondria [10], lysosome [11], and endoplasmic reticulum [12].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a promising fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted considerable interest for their fascinating optical and biocompatibility properties [13]. Recently, several CDs have been successfully used for polarity measurement in living systems [14–16]. Li group presented 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine (NAP)-derived CDs with large Stokes shift for imaging cancer cell polarity [17]. Our group developed a polymer CDs for imaging dynamic changes in polarity during autophagy [18]. Unfortunately, these CDs were not possible to distinguish the local polarity changes within distinct organelles from the whole intracellular area. To better meet local sensing requirements, surface chemical modification strategy was generally adopted for subcellular targeting [19]. However, the modification process is often complex, time-consuming, and inefficient. Therefore, great efforts have been made to fabricate CDs with intrinsic organelle-targeting properties for detecting polarity.

The shape memory effect, a phenomenon in which a material switches to its original shape upon exposure with external stimuli, has been used for fabricating specific smart and controllable materials [20]. Correspondingly, function memory effect has been first proposed by our group [21] and has been applied to guide the synthesis of organelle-targeting CDs. As an example, abundant free sulfhydryl groups of precursor L-cysteine retained on the surface of CDs has been proven to be essential for Golgi targeting [22]. Amino-rich CDs with self-targeting ability toward mitochondria benefited from the hydrophobic regions and positive surface charges were engineered using perylene tetracarboxylic dianhydride, polyethyleneimine and ammonium fluoride as precursors [23]. In addition to above mentioned, metal elements [24] and active structure [21] could also be memorized and inherited, which provided a facile way for obtaining multifunctional CDs.

Golgi apparatus, as an acidic organelle (pH 6.0–6.7), has important functions in processing, sorting and moving new synthesized proteins and lipids from the endoplasmic reticulum to specific destination [25]. Changes of microenvironment (e.g.,viscosity, polarity, pH, temperature) in Golgi apparatus may cause morphologic abnormalities and ultimately result in a wide range of diseases [26]. High Golgi polarity was closely associated with depression-like behavior and acute kidney injury, suggesting that polarity may be served as a potential early indicator for the occurrence and development of disease [27,28]. These small molecular organic probes hold advantages in imaging of Golgi polarity variation, but the utility is generally limited by their poor water solubility and photostability. Researches on CDs-based novel probes with Golgi apparatus-specific polarity-sensitive properties for long-term tracking is one to keep an eye on.

The design of Golgi-localized small molecules probes generally involves basic nitrogenous groups (such as amino, pyridyl, etc.) which can be recruited in the acidic cellular compartments owing to the “acid-base interaction” mechanism [29,30]. In this work, we designed a Golgi apparatus-targeting CDs with functional amino groups based on memory effect. As depicted in Fig. 1 , a set of CDs was synthesized in water or ethanol under different solvothermal conditions from various carbon sources as precursor molecules, followed by separation with silica column chromatography or dialysis. They are designated hereafter as CD-1, CD-2, CD-3, CD-4, CD-5, and CD-6, respectively. The resulting CDs possessed free amino groups in benzene ring and diamine derivatives were essential for Golgi targeting. Meanwhile, this probe could be applied for tracking Golgi apparatus polarity changes in living systems, and the polarity of the Golgi apparatus increased gradually with the process of apoptosis.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer (Japan) and a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Functional group analysis was conducted on an IR Prestige-21 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Shimadzu, Japan) spectrometer, and elemental analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, Thermo, USA) instrument. Fluorescence lifetimes of the as-obtained CDs were estimated on a Jobin Yvon FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, France). 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were recorded a Bruker 400 MHz superconducting Fourier NMR spectrometer (Germany). Fluorescence imaging was conducted by confocal scanning microscopy (Japan, Olympus DSU).

p-Phenylenediamine (PPD), ethylenediamine (EDA), doxorubicin (DOX), polystyrene (PS, MW = 35000), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 10000), polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW = 8000), poly(vinylalcohol) (PVA, MW = 13000), and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China) were used as received. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Metal salts and organic solvents were purchased from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). 4-Aminoazobenzene was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Japan). Dimethyl yellow was purchased by Shanghai Yuanhang Reagent Factory (China). RPMI and DMEM cultures were purchased from Hyclone (Thermo, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Gibco (Australia). BODIPY TR C5-ceramide (Golgi-Tracker Red), CCK-8, and Hanks’ balanced salt solution (with Ca2+ and Mg2+) were obtained from Beyotime Biochem. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NBD-C6-ceramide (Golgi Green) was purchased from iGene Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China).

First, 0.11 g of PPD and 535 μL of EDA were added into a 25-mL Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave, followed by the addition of 3 mL of ethanol as the solvent. Second, the mixed solution was heated at 180 °C for 10 h. When the reaction was completed, the mixture was separated and purified by silica-gel column chromatography, affording two fluorescent samples (i.e., CDs-1 g and CDs-1y, respectively). In addition, CDs-1r was separated by continuous dialysis using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da). CDs-2 and CDs-3 were synthesized from a mixture of 0.110 g of PPD and 676 μL of 1,3-propanediamine or 816 μL of 1,4-diaminobutane in ethanol at 160 °C for 10 h, respectively. CDs-4 was prepared using 0.11 g of PPD and 0.2 g of citric acid in water as the solvent at 180 °C for 10 h. CDs-5 was prepared from an aqueous mixture of 0.1 g 4-aminoazobenzene and 275 μL of EDA at 220 °C for 16 h. CDs-6 was obtained by treating the mixture of 0.112 g of dimethyl yellow and 275 μL of EDA at 220 °C for 16 h by using water as the solvent. CDs-2, CDs-3, CDs-4, CDs-5, and CDs-6 prepared with the above methods were purified by a silica-gel column using a mixture of methanol and dichloromethane as the eluent.

70 μL (66.7 mg) of acetic acid was dissolved in 3.0 mL of anhydrous DMF. EDC (191.0 mg) and NHS (115.0 mg) were then added into the above solution. The mixed solution was stirred at room temperature (25 °C) for 2 h. Subsequently, another 2 mL of a DMF solution containing CDs-1y (20.0 mg) was injected into the above resulting mixture. Then, the mixture was stirred for another 16 h. After the reaction, the obtained products were purified by silica-gel column chromatography.

Firstly, 20 mg of folic acid was dissolved in 3.0 mL of anhydrous DMF. EDC (19.2 mg) and NHS (11.5 mg) were added into the above solution. Next, the mixed solution was stirred at room temperature (25 °C) for 20 min. Then, another 2 mL of a DMF solution containing CDs-1y (20.0 mg) was injected into the above resulting mixture. The mixture was subsequently stirred for another 16 h. After the reaction, the formed precipitate was removed by centrifugation. Following the procedure, DMF was removed by vacuum evaporation, and the obtained product was redissolved in water, which was further purified by dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of golgi-polarity changes during apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, A549 cells were plated in glass-bottom dishes with a density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1 and incubated for 24 h before use. Next, 1 mL of RPMI 1640 with 2% FBS containing 50 μg mL−1 CDs-1y was used to replace the previous medium in the dishes. The cells were cultured for another 4 h. Then, the medium was removed, and 1 mL of fresh medium with 2% FBS containing DOX (10 μg mL−1) was injected into dishes. The cells were later cultured for another 6 h or 12 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed twice with RPMI. After adding 500 μL of the RPMI medium in the dishes, the cells were observed by confocal microscopy at different channels.

Detailed experimental procedures of quantum yield measurements, cell culture, cytotoxicity investigation, imaging co-localization, and dynamic observation of the Golgi apparatus in cell division and apoptosis can be found in Supporting Material.

Mixture of CDs-1 was synthesized by the solvothermal method using PPD and EDA as precursors to achieve amino-terminated CDs. Then, silica column chromatography was employed to fractionate CDs-1 and three distinctive fluorescence regions (red, yellow, green) under UV lamp were collected and labeled as CDs-1r, CDs-1y, CDs-1g. The TEM images revealed that all the CDs were uniform and quasi-spherical nanoparticles with average diameter of about 15.9 ± 2.64, 3.38 ± 0.89, and 5.57 ± 0.96 nm, respectively (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). HRTEM images of single CDs illustrated that only CDs-1y exhibited crystallinity with lattice spacings of 0.34 and 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon, respectively, while CDs-1g and CDs-1r were typical amorphous CDs [31]. Subsequently, the optical properties of CDs-1 fractions were investigated systematically. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs displayed a similar absorption in the UV region of 200–300 nm. A sharp peak was observed at 240 nm with a shoulder at 290 nm, which originated from the π-π* transition of C = C and C = N bonds in aromatic core. Different behavior was observed in the lower-energy region (350–800 nm) which was commonly assigned to the surface states. In detail, the CDs-1g and CDs-1y exhibited specific absorption peaks at 392 and 402 nm as well as the CDs-1r displayed two peaks at 385 nm and 525 nm, which were linked to the excitation peak, suggesting that functional groups on the surface of CDs-1y, CDs-1g, and CDs-1r were distinct (Fig. S3). Unlike many other reported CDs in the literature [32], CDs-1 exhibited an excitation-independent emission behavior (Fig. S4). Taking series of organic dyes as reference, the quantum yields of CDs-1y was 24.8%, while those for CDs-1r and CDs-1g decreased to 2.6% and 2.5% in an aqueous solution (Fig. S5 and Table S1). At the same time, the average FL lifetimes were 3.05, 2.63, and 1.70 ns for CDs-1g, CDs-1y, and CDs-1r, respectively (Fig. S6).

The chemical composition of the three samples were further investigated by FTIR and XPS studies. Full survey XPS spectra confirmed that all CDs composed C, N, and O atoms (Fig. S7). The content of nitrogen and oxygen of CDs-1y (7.20% and 10.3%) were found to be higher than that of another two CDs, which may be responsible for relatively high QY of CDs-1y (Table S2). FTIR peaks at 3500–3250 cm−1 revealed that all samples exhibited abundant hydrophilic groups such as N-H and O-H on their surface (Fig. S8), ensuring the good water solubility of CDs. Notably, the C-N = C structure was formed by the condensation between the amino groups of starting materials, as evidenced by the vibration peaks at 1628 cm−1 and 1493 cm−1 as well as deconvoluted peaks of C1s at 285.2 eV (C–N) and 288.2 eV (C = N/C = O) in the high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. S9) [33]. Pyridinic N (398.8 eV), amino N (399.5 eV), and pyrrolic N (400.1 eV) were also observed in the high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S9B). The O 1 s peak consisted of two bands at ∼ 531.4 and ∼ 532.7 eV, which was attributed to C = O and C–O/O–H (Fig. S9C). Furthermore, 1H and 13C NMR spectra of graphitic CDs-1y further confirmed the presence of primary amino groups in both aliphatic and aromatic amines (Fig. S10 and Fig. S11).

For practical applications, it is important to investigate the stability of these CDs. We compared photostability of as-prepared CDs-1 with that of the commonly used organic molecule FITC during high-power Xe lamp continuous irradiation. The fluorescence of FITC dramatically decreased due to severe photobleaching, while CDs-1y preserved highly stable optical property in comparison with CDs-1g and CDs-1r. Specifically, the fluorescence of CDs-1y still maintained over 60% and less than 10% were stayed for CDs-1g and CDs-1r within 80 min of irradiation (Fig. S12). The intensity of characteristic absorption bands about FITC (520 nm), CDs-1g (392 nm), and CDs-1r (525 nm) decreased gradually, accompanied by negligible changes for CDs-1y (Fig. S13). This may be a consequence of difference in surface-state of three samples because the intrinsic state of the CDs was more photostable [34]. Considering the good photostability, the as-obtained CDs-1y were selected for further applications.

Additionally, the effect of pH, hydrogen peroxide, ionic strength, cations, and anions on the fluorescence of the CDs-1y was further studied, and results shown that it was stable in the pH range of 5 to 8 or with high ionic strengths and hydrogen peroxide. Common cations and biologically relevant anions did not change the fluorescence intensity of CDs-1y (Fig. S14). Optical characteristics of CDs-1y dissolved in solvents with different polarities were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 , CDs-1y exhibited blue, green, yellow-green, and yellow emissions under excitation by a 365-nm UV lamp when dissolved in solvents such as toluene, chloroform, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, methanol, and water, indicating that the solvatochromic shifts (Δλ) of CDs-1y increased with the increase in the polarity, where slight absorption peak shifts were observed (Fig. S15) because the adsorption of light usually occurs in about 10−15 s whereas the solvent relaxation happens in 10–100 ps. Therefore, the ground state and the excited state of CDs-1y may be in the same local environment during the entire absorption process, while the solvent relaxed state represented by the emission spectra of CDs-1y are sensitive to solvent polarity. Notably, CDs-1y possessed tunable emissions correlated to the polarity parameter (Δf) in the 490–563 nm range (Fig. 2C and Table S3). This polarity-dependent emission was similar with the “solvatochromism” of fluorescent organic small molecules (OSMs), typically originating from the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and hydrogen-bonding effect [35]. Thus, solvent molecules, with different dipole moments, has exerted a considerable effect on stabilization energies of the excited states of CDs-1y. Furthermore, CDs-1y also emitted tunable colors when they were dispersed into different solid polymers, wherein polymer matrixes were considered as “solid solvents” (Fig. 2D). CDs-1y exhibited emissions at 486, 504, 530, and 554 nm in PS, PVP, PEG, and PVA respectively, indicating that the polymers around the CDs exerted interactions like those of solvents and the surface electronic structures of CDs-1y were changed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL imaging of the Golgi apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, CDs-1y displayed good biocompatibility (Figs. S16 and S17), making it suitable for the live cell imaging. Interestingly, confocal imaging demonstrated that the bright blue fluorescence was accumulated in Golgi apparatus, revealing that CDs-1y could selectively target Golgi apparatus in A549, HepG2, HeLa, and HUVEC cells (Fig. S18). A clear colocalization of CDs-1y (blue) and a commercially available Golgi-staining dye BODIPY TR ceramide (red) was observed, suggesting that CDs-1y was indeed transported to the Golgi apparatus (Fig. 3 A&B). It should be noted that CDs-1g and CDs-1r also exhibited the Golgi apparatus-targeting ability (Fig. S19). The CDs-1y stained cells were then incubated with nuclear dye Hoechst 33258. The images clearly demonstrated that the Golgi apparatus was located close to the nucleus (Fig. S20).

To check whether the specific interaction of CDs-1y toward the Golgi apparatus originates from the N-functional groups, a series of CDs-x (x = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) from other precursors were further synthesized (Fig. 1B and Fig. S21). As expected, CDs-2 and CDs-3 with similar molecular groups to CDs-1y (N-H, 3370–3410 cm−1; C-N = C, 1635–1645 cm−1) (Fig. S22A&B) localized to the Golgi apparatus in A549 cells (Fig. 3C and Fig. S23). In contrast to CDs-1y, CDs-4 did not target to Golgi apparatus by itself, which was attributed to the carboxyl groups on the surface (-C = O, 1705 cm−1) (Fig. S22C). Given this, we reasoned that the amide bond and acidic structure did not contribute to the Golgi-targeting ability. Furthermore, amino groups on benzene ring of raw material were investigated. CDs-5 and CDs-6 were synthesized using 4-aminoazobenzene and dimethyl yellow as starting material, where the key difference between them is the amino group. Colocalization experiment revealed that only CDs-5 targeted the Golgi apparatus. Above results confirmed that free amino groups in benzene ring and diamines were essential for the formation of CDs with Golgi-targeting ability. To further verify this, we blocked the amine functionality of the CDs-1y with acetic acid based on the EDC/NHS chemistry. Blue-shifted emission and absorption bands were found upon conjugation with acetic acid (Fig. S24A). And the successful conjugation of acetic acid to CDs-1y was confirmed by FTIR because the obvious characteristic peak for stretching vibration of C = O bond in amide group was observed at 1705 cm−1 (Fig. S24B). Fluorescence image showed that the fluorescence was widely distributed in the cytoplasm, with no apparent localization to specific organelle (Fig. S24C). Together, these data suggested that the importance of amino group in the CDs design for Golgi apparatus-targeting, which may be protonated at proper pH-values and therefore accumulated in the compartments, which effectively resulted its retention.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological dynamics of the Golgi apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the good photostability and biocompatibility, we then evaluated the feasibility of CDs-1y for tracking Golgi apparatus dynamics. CDs-1y was introduced into the cells by incubation for 12 h to examine the Golgi-targeting ability during cell division. CDs-1y has been localizing in the Golgi apparatus and retained in the daughter cells’ Golgi throughout two cell cycles, accompanying with the significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S25). More importantly, we demonstrated the reassemble process of the Golgi apparatus from small fragments with the aid of CDs-1y label in daughter cells (Fig. S26).

Subsequently, CDs-1y was further assessed for visualizing Golgi complex disassembly during apoptosis (Fig. S27). In cells induced apoptotic by ultraviolet light, negligible effect on the Golgi apparatus morphology was observed, while treatment with ammonium chloride, the Golgi structure of cells changed obviously and underwent irreversible fragmentation. The results confirmed that CDs-1y could be served as excellent tools to monitor the Golgi-specific dynamics associated with the conversion between physiological and pathological states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polarity imaging of the Golgi apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: Previous work revealed that the Golgi apparatus at the normal states presented a hydrophobic microenvironment compared with that in water solution, whereas a significant blue-shift of the fluorescence emission reflected lower local polarity. To assess the use of CDs-1y for tracking polarity changes during apoptosis, DOX treatment leaded to long-wavelength emission accompanied by fluorescence intensity increase from Fura340 to CY3 channels, indicating a polarity-dependent of CDs-1y across the apoptosis (Fig. 4 ). This increased polarity of the Golgi apparatus could be attributed to the substantial recruitment of polar proteins in the Golgi apparatus.

In addition, we found that CDs-5 can also be applied for polarity sensing based on the fluorescence intensity rather than solvatochromic shift. As the solvent polarity increased, the fluorescence intensity was decreased significantly (Fig. 5 A). To better understand the solvent polarity effect, binary mixtures of 1,4-dioxane and water were selected as solvent systems to investigate (Table S4). The linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of CDs-5 and the solvent polarity (Δf) from 0.243 to 0.306 fitting to the Boltzmann equation was observed (Fig. 5B and Fig. S28). Additionally, the CDs-5 showed excellent photostability, thermostability, and chemical stability (Fig. S29). Although the fluorescence intensity of CDs-5 decreased at both low (pH < 4) and high pH (pH > 8) in the buffer solution, it preserved high pH stability over the physiologically relevant pH range of 4–8.

The effect of UV irradiation on local polarity in living cells was further evaluated using CDs-5. Fluorescence imaging experiments confirmed that CDs-5 could localize to Golgi apparatus effectively and UV irradiation did not affect the structure and functional at first. After a 12 h incubation, dissociation of the Golgi apparatus occurred and CDs-5 was released into the medium. Correspondingly, the fluorescence intensity was significantly decrease, suggesting that an increase in the local polarity during apoptosis (Fig. 5C&D). These data indicated that CDs-5 has strong affinity for the Golgi apparatus and can also be used for monitoring local polarity under diverse physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-1y modified with FA for imaging the Golgi of cancer cell
ce:section.ce:para: Folate receptor, which highly overexpressed in many malignant tumors, has been recognized as one of the most well-studied targets for drug delivery. To endow CDs-1y for imaging Golgi apparatus polarity change in cancer cells, we modified CDs-1y with folate (FA) through EDC/NHS reaction because the high affinity to FR may facilitate CDs-1y entering cancer cells (Fig. 6 A). It should be noted that the amino group in CDs-1y is critical for Golgi apparatus targeting, and therefore the amounts for conjugation must be controlled precisely. FA-modified CDs-1y (CDs-1y-FA) displayed a shift in the absorption to shorter wavelengths and exhibited bright green fluorescence (Fig. S30). FTIR confirmed the appearance of the C = O stretching frequency at 1701 cm−1, and the introduction of an amide C = O stretch at 1658 cm−1 with an additional N-H bending mode (Fig. S31).

The ability of CDs-1y-FA to target the Golgi of cancer cells was further evaluated. Normal cell HUVEC and cancer cells HeLa were selected to be incubated with CDs-1y-FA, respectively. Upon excitation, HeLa cells exhibited strong and stable green fluorescence after incubation for 30 min, whereas HUVEC cells showed negligible signal (Fig. 6B&C&D). In addition, with Golgi apparatus staining dye, a clear colocalization was observed (Fig. S32). Folate-mediated endocytosis of CDs-1y-FA can offer a novel route for designing multistage-targeted nanoplatform for guiding anti-cancer drug delivery and sensing the local polarity change.

In conclusion, we have developed fluorescent probe amino-terminated graphitic CDs for Golgi apparatus imaging based on function memory effect and revealed the relationship between surface groups and the organelle-targeting ability, providing a novel general strategy for designing Golgi-staining CDs-based fluorescent probe. Notably, this CDs exhibited high biocompatibility, photostability, and sensitivity to polarity, thus making it suitable for visualizing polarity changes in the Golgi apparatus microenvironment. Furthermore, we found that Golgi apparatus polarity tended to be higher during apoptosis. And a long-time tracking the dynamic change of Golgi morphology during cell division or induced by chemical treatment was realized using CDs. FA-modified CDs effectively targeted cancer cells and accumulated in Golgi apparatus for identifying the polarity. Together, we believed that the amino-terminated CDs can be served as a promising tool for subcellular drug delivery. Future research is required to Golgi apparatus polarity-related cell biology and pathology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976144 and 22134005), and Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.145613.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
